rio-de-janeiro.title: Rio de Janeiro
rio-de-janeiro.gettingThere: Fly to Galeao International Airport (GIG) — major hub with direct flights from North America, Europe, and across South America. 30-60 min taxi or bus to Copacabana/Ipanema. Santos Dumont (SDU) handles domestic flights and is closer to the city center.

essentials.gettingAround: Metro covers Zona Sul and Centro well. Uber and 99 rideshare widely used. Buses extensive but confusing for visitors. BRT for longer routes.
essentials.language: Portuguese, limited English in tourist areas

sao-paulo.title: Sao Paulo
sao-paulo.gettingThere: Fly to Guarulhos International Airport (GRU) — Brazil's busiest airport. Airport Express bus to Paulista ~R$50 (1 hr). Congonhas (CGH) handles domestic shuttles and is closer to the center.
highlights: Vila Madalena street art (Beco do Batman)

essentials.gettingAround: Metro is clean and efficient. Uber and 99 essential for the sprawling city. Traffic is legendary — avoid rush hours.
trainConnections: Bus to Rio de Janeiro (6 hr, ~R$120)
essentials.language: Portuguese, some English in upscale areas

salvador.title: Salvador
salvador.gettingThere: Fly to Salvador Airport (SSA). Uber or taxi to Pelourinho ~R$60 (30 min). Direct flights from major Brazilian cities and some international routes.
highlights: Church of Sao Francisco golden interior

essentials.gettingAround: Pelourinho is walkable but hilly. Uber for longer trips. Lacerda Elevator connects Upper and Lower City. Be cautious at night outside tourist areas.

florianopolis.title: Florianopolis
florianopolis.gettingThere: Fly to Florianopolis Airport (FLN). Uber or taxi to beaches ~R$40-80 depending on destination. Car rental recommended for exploring the island.
highlights: Santo Antonio de Lisboa fishing village

essentials.gettingAround: Car rental strongly recommended — island is large and buses are slow. Uber available but can be scarce at remote beaches.
essentials.language: Portuguese, some English in tourist areas

iguazu-falls.title: Iguazu Falls
iguazu-falls.gettingThere: Fly to Foz do Iguacu Airport (IGU). Taxi or Uber to hotels ~R$30-50 (15 min). National Park bus from city center runs regularly.
highlights: Argentine side upper and lower trails

essentials.gettingAround: Taxi or Uber between hotels and park. Park bus included with entrance fee. Consider visiting both Brazilian and Argentine sides (bring passport).
essentials.language: Portuguese and Spanish, some English at tourist sites

brasilia.title: Brasilia
brasilia.gettingThere: Fly to Brasilia Airport (BSB) — major domestic hub. Uber or taxi to hotel row ~R$30-50 (20 min). Well-connected to all major Brazilian cities.

essentials.gettingAround: City designed for cars — distances are large. Uber essential. Metro limited but connects airport to center. Walking between sights on Esplanada dos Ministerios is possible.

manaus.title: Manaus
manaus.gettingThere: Fly to Manaus Airport (MAO). No road connections to most of Brazil — flying is the only practical option. Uber or taxi to center ~R$40 (20 min).

essentials.gettingAround: City center walkable. Uber for wider areas. Jungle tours arranged through lodges or agencies — multi-day recommended. Boat trips along the river.
essentials.language: Portuguese, very limited English even in tourist areas

recife.title: Recife
recife.gettingThere: Fly to Recife Airport (REC). Uber or taxi to Boa Viagem ~R$30 (20 min). Direct flights from major Brazilian cities and some European routes.

essentials.gettingAround: Uber and 99 widely available. Metro connects airport to center. Boa Viagem area walkable. Day trip to Olinda by taxi or bus (30 min).
trainConnections: Flight to Fernando de Noronha (1 hr, ~R$600)

paraty.title: Paraty
paraty.tagline: Colonial Jewel on the Costa Verde
paraty.gettingThere: No airport — reach by bus from Rio (4.5 hr, ~R$80) or Sao Paulo (5.5 hr, ~R$100). Car rental offers flexibility to explore the coast. Scenic coastal drive along BR-101.
highlights: UNESCO historic center with cobblestone streets

essentials.gettingAround: Historic center is car-free and walkable. Boat trips to beaches and islands. Taxi or rental car for outlying beaches. Bikes rentable for nearby areas.
essentials.airport: Nearest: Rio de Janeiro (GIG) or Sao Paulo (GRU)
trainConnections: Bus to Rio de Janeiro (4.5 hr, ~R$80)
essentials.language: Portuguese, some English in tourist businesses

ouro-preto.title: Ouro Preto
ouro-preto.gettingThere: Nearest airport is Belo Horizonte (CNF), then 2-hour bus ride (~R$50). Direct buses from Belo Horizonte bus station run frequently. Scenic mountain road.
highlights: Igreja de Sao Francisco de Assis
Historic gold mines (Mina do Chico Rei)

essentials.gettingAround: Compact and walkable but extremely hilly — wear sturdy shoes. Streets are steep cobblestone. Taxis for outlying mines and sights.

jericoacoara.title: Jericoacoara
jericoacoara.gettingThere: Fly to Jericoacoara Airport (JJD) or Fortaleza (FOR, then 5-hr transfer). 4WD transfer from Jijoca de Jericoacoara through sand dunes required. Pre-book transfers.
highlights: Sunset from Duna do Por do Sol

essentials.gettingAround: Village is car-free with sandy streets — go barefoot. Buggy or 4WD for lagoons and outlying areas. Horse and donkey transport still common.
trainConnections: Flight Fortaleza to Sao Paulo (3.5 hr, ~R$350)
essentials.language: Portuguese, limited English, some Italian and French from expats

fernando-de-noronha.title: Fernando de Noronha
fernando-de-noronha.gettingThere: Fly from Recife (REC, 1 hr) or Natal (NAT, 1.5 hr). Daily environmental tax (~R$100/day). Limited flights and visitor caps — book well ahead.
highlights: Baia dos Porcos & Morro Dois Irmaos
Sea turtle nesting at Praia do Leao

essentials.gettingAround: Buggy rental (~R$250/day) is standard. Island is small but hilly. Some beaches require hiking. Electric buggies now available. Reserve Atalaia trail spots online in advance.

bonito.title: Bonito
bonito.gettingThere: Fly to Campo Grande (CGR) then 5-hour bus or rental car. Some direct flights to Bonito Airport (BYO) seasonally. Car rental gives best flexibility.
highlights: Gruta do Lago Azul blue cave

essentials.gettingAround: Car rental recommended — attractions spread across the region. All activities must be booked through licensed agencies (voucher system). Book popular activities days ahead in high season.
trainConnections: Flight Campo Grande to Sao Paulo (1.5 hr, ~R$300)
Bus to Foz do Iguacu (via Campo Grande, 12 hr total)
essentials.language: Portuguese, limited English at tour agencies

travelTips.currency: Brazilian Real (BRL / R$). Cards widely accepted in cities. Carry cash for smaller towns, beach vendors, and street food. ATMs (Banco 24h) available at airports and malls.
travelTips.visaFree: US citizens need an eVisa (apply online, ~$80, valid 10 years). EU, UK, Australia, Canada — visa-free for 90 days. Check current requirements before travel.
travelTips.bestOverallMonths: April to October for most destinations (dry season varies by region). December to March for beaches and Carnival. June to September for Amazon low-water season and Pantanal wildlife.
travelTips.winterNote: Brazil has no harsh winter. Southern regions (Florianopolis, Bonito) are cooler Jun-Aug (10-20C). Northeast stays warm year-round (25-32C). Amazon is hot and humid always.
travelTips.simCard: Buy a Claro, Vivo, or TIM prepaid SIM at any airport for ~R$30-50/month with good 4G/5G coverage in cities. Coverage drops in Amazon and remote beaches.
travelTips.transport: Domestic flights via GOL, LATAM, and Azul connect all major cities. Long-distance buses are comfortable (leito class = reclining beds). Uber and 99 ride-hailing in all cities. Rent a car for coastal and rural exploration.
